title: Stop Using Opus For Everything — A Practical Guide to Opus vs Sonnet
tagline: After months of real-world use across IT, documentation, and office work, the conclusion is clear: Opus is overkill for 85–90% of daily tasks. Here's exactly where the line sits.
content:
I've been using both Claude Opus and Sonnet extensively for work — IT and sysadmin tasks, Intune policies, documentation, general office tasks — and I've reached a simple conclusion: **Opus is overkill for the vast majority of daily work.**

Here's the breakdown after months of real-world use.

**When Sonnet is all you need**

**Everyday office work** — emails, reports, summaries, meeting notes, status updates. Sonnet handles all of this perfectly. Using Opus here is like hiring a linguistics professor to write a sticky note.

**Following direct instructions** — "Take this config, put it in the as-built doc, update the checklist." No ambiguity, no nuance required. Sonnet executes these flawlessly.

**Simple Q&A and explanations** — "What does this setting do?" "Summarise this document." Sonnet gives clean, accurate answers without needing the extra horsepower.

**Mixed-language conversations** — If you're working across multiple languages or code-switching between technical jargon and natural language, Sonnet handles it fine for normal conversation.

**Standard documentation** — Writing SOPs, templates, basic technical docs. Sonnet's output quality is more than sufficient.

**When Opus is worth it**

**Complex analysis with many moving parts** — When you need to hold multiple variables in context simultaneously and reason through dependencies. A concrete example: figuring out why a custom Intune policy conflicts with a security baseline when the answer requires cross-referencing ADMX templates, OMA-URI paths, and JSON configs simultaneously.

**Parsing complex or messy technical formats** — ADMX templates, nested OMA-URI strings, deeply structured JSON with interdependencies. Opus doesn't just find the information — it's noticeably better at making sense of what it found.

**Nuanced communication** — Writing a delicate client rejection, navigating political subtext in corporate correspondence, anything where every word carries weight and context is everything.

**Long session context retention** — If you've been working for an hour, configured 20 policies, and then ask the model to update a document referencing earlier work, Opus is more reliable at retaining what happened at the start of the conversation.

**Meta-tasks** — Using AI to plan how to use AI. Prompt engineering, architecture planning, breaking down complex problems into sub-tasks. This is where Opus earns its place most clearly.

**The elephant in the room: it's not about coding**

The AI community is obsessed with coding benchmarks — SWE-bench scores, HumanEval percentages. But the real mass impact of AI isn't replacing developers. It's the billions of hours spent daily on office work: reports, emails, data entry, document management, compliance paperwork.

Coders will adapt — they're technical by definition. The person copy-pasting between Excel and SAP for eight hours a day is where the real displacement happens. And like chimney sweeps and radio technicians before them, those roles won't disappear with a bang — organisations will quietly stop hiring for them.

Yet almost nobody benchmarks models on "wrote a convincing stakeholder update" or "correctly interpreted a confusing policy document." It's harder to measure, so it doesn't get measured. That doesn't make it less important — it makes it more overlooked.

**The practical argument: usage limits**

On the Pro plan, Sonnet's usage limits are significantly higher than Opus. You can work all day without hitting a wall. With Opus, you'll run into limits fairly quickly — especially during long sessions with large context windows.

The strategic approach: default to Sonnet, escalate to Opus deliberately when the task genuinely warrants it. Reserve your Opus quota for the work where the reasoning depth actually changes the outcome.

title: The One Prompt Structure That Outperforms Everything Else
tagline: Role. Task. Format. Three words that eliminate ambiguity and guarantee higher-quality outputs every time — if you only remember one framework, make it this one.
content:
Every LLM needs three things to produce a great output: who it should be, what it should do, and how the result should look. Most prompts provide one of these. The best prompts provide all three explicitly.

The structure is simple: **/ROLE: [persona] / TASK: [specific objective] / FORMAT: [exact output structure]**

Example in practice: "/ROLE: Senior Financial Analyst / TASK: Summarise the key risks in this quarterly report / FORMAT: Three bullet points, max 20 words each, ordered by severity."

**Why each element matters independently:**

**Role** activates a different reasoning register. Asking Claude to respond "as a Senior Financial Analyst" doesn't just change the vocabulary — it changes the implicit assumptions, the level of precision, and what the model considers worth flagging. The persona is a filter on the entire response.

**Task** needs to be specific enough that there is only one reasonable interpretation. "Summarise this" has dozens of valid interpretations. "Identify the three biggest risks and explain why each one could affect operating margin" has one.

**Format** removes the negotiation phase. When you don't specify format, the model chooses one — and it may not match your use case. A three-bullet executive summary and a detailed narrative analysis are both valid responses to the same task. Specifying format means you get what you need on the first attempt.

The practical test: if you read your prompt and can imagine two people producing meaningfully different outputs, the prompt is under-specified. Tighten the Role, Task, or Format until only one interpretation remains.

title: How to Set Operating Rules for Claude Before Any Analysis
tagline: Treat your AI like a new contractor: brief it on constraints, data standards, and what to do when something looks wrong. Here's a reusable instruction set.
content:
The most common failure mode in AI-assisted analysis isn't hallucination — it's the model confidently applying generic assumptions to domain-specific problems. The fix is a set of operating instructions delivered at the start of every relevant session.

Think of it as an SLA between you and the model. You define the rules; the model operates within them. The discipline of writing these instructions also forces clarity on what your analytical standards actually are.

**A reusable instruction template for financial and operational analysis:**

**1. Always reference the source definitions first.** If you've provided contract terms, data dictionaries, or methodology documents, the model must use those — not generic definitions. Conflicts between your definitions and standard accounting definitions should be flagged, not silently resolved.

**2. Confirm data completeness before producing output.** If the dataset appears incomplete, inconsistent, or covers an unexpected time period, flag it before proceeding. Partial analysis presented as complete analysis is worse than no analysis.

**3. Use a consistent output hierarchy.** For P&L work: Total Gross Revenue → Adjusted Gross Revenue → Total Costs by category → Net Income → Operating Margin. Lock the structure so every output is comparable.

**4. Show the formula for every KPI.** Not just the result — the inputs, the formula, and the result. This makes outputs auditable and catches errors before they reach a deck.

**5. Flag anomalies before finalising.** Negative margins, unusual ratios, variances beyond a defined threshold — surface these explicitly rather than burying them in the output.

**6. Never fabricate figures.** If data is missing, state what is missing and what is needed. An honest gap is always preferable to a plausible estimate.

**One operational lesson worth noting:** new conversations are more token-efficient than continuing long threads. When a session gets unwieldy, save the key context, start fresh, and paste the summary at the top. You get a cleaner model state and faster responses.

title: Give Claude a Map of Your Files Without Sharing the Files
tagline: Claude can't read your SharePoint — but one terminal command gives it structural awareness of your entire drive in seconds.
content:
Claude has no direct access to SharePoint, network drives, or local file systems. But structural awareness — knowing what files exist, where they live, and how they're organised — is often enough to dramatically improve the relevance of its outputs.

The tactic: run the `tree` command in Windows Terminal to export a full directory map, then feed that text file to Claude at the start of your session.

**The command:**

`tree "C:\Users\YourUsername\Legends\Legends Global Insights - Documents\Insights Team" /F > sharepoint_tree.txt`

This produces a plain text file at `C:\Users\username\sharepoint_tree.txt` listing every folder and filename in the specified directory. Paste or upload it into Claude before your first question.

**What this gives you:** Claude won't know the contents of those files, but it will know they exist. It can reference specific filenames, suggest which documents are likely relevant to your task, avoid asking you to locate files it can already see in the map, and structure requests around your actual file organisation rather than a generic assumption.

**Where this works best:** Teams with descriptive, consistent file naming conventions. If your SharePoint looks like `Final_v3_USE THIS_REVISED.xlsx`, the tree won't help much. The value is proportional to the quality of your naming discipline.

**Extending the tactic:** The `tree` command works for any directory. Run it on project folders, data export directories, analysis output folders — wherever you want Claude to have structural awareness without full file access. It's a low-effort, high-value workaround until native file integration is available.

title: The 5-Category Command Library: 28 Shortcuts That Change How Claude Responds
tagline: Slash commands are semantic anchors, not UI buttons. Understanding the difference is what separates users who get consistent results from those who don't.
content:
These shortcuts work because language models are highly attuned to instructional framing placed at the beginning of a text block. They're not functions — they're signals that prime the model's interpretation of everything that follows.

**Category 1 — Simplification**

`/ELI5` — Explain like I'm five. Forces plain language, analogies, zero jargon.
`/TLDR` — Compress any text to its essential points.
`/BRIEFLY` — Maximum brevity. One to three sentences.
`/EXEC SUMMARY` — Key points only, executive register, no elaboration.

**Category 2 — Structure**

`/STEP-BY-STEP` — Sequential reasoning. Each step explicit before the next.
`/CHECKLIST` — Convert any output to an actionable verification list.
`/FORMAT AS [table/JSON/markdown]` — Specify the exact output container.
`/ROLE: TASK: FORMAT:` — The master framework. Define all three and eliminate ambiguity.

**Category 3 — Persona**

`/ACT AS [role]` — Activates domain-specific reasoning and vocabulary.
`/AUDIENCE [demographic]` — Calibrates complexity and assumed knowledge.
`/TONE [formal/urgent/analytical]` — Controls emotional register.
`/JARGON` — Maximum technical density for expert audiences.

**Category 4 — Analysis**

`/COMPARE` — Side-by-side structured comparison, usually a table.
`/SWOT` — Full strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats.
`/FIRST PRINCIPLES` — Strip all assumptions; rebuild from fundamentals.
`/PITFALLS` — Surface edge cases and failure modes proactively.
`/METRICS MODE` — Express everything in quantitative terms.
`/CHAIN OF THOUGHT` — Show reasoning steps before the conclusion. Essential for complex logic or maths.

**Category 5 — Quality Control**

`/NO AUTOPILOT` — Bans generic, safe, corporate-language outputs.
`/EVAL-SELF` — Forces the model to critique its own response.
`/GUARDRAIL [constraint]` — Sets hard limits (e.g. "do not mention competitors").
`/REFLECTIVE MODE` — Identifies blind spots in the model's own reasoning.

**Three rules for effective use:** Place commands at the very start of the prompt. Stack a maximum of two to three compatible commands — contradictory combinations (e.g. `/ELI5` + `/JARGON`) produce confused output. And remember: shortcuts amplify context, they don't replace it. A poorly defined prompt with a shortcut produces a well-formatted poorly defined output.

title: How to Build a Senior Data Analyst Into Your Prompt
tagline: A four-layer analytical framework that transforms any dataset upload into a structured, business-grade analysis — with statistical rigour and executive communication built in.
content:
Upload a CSV and ask "what do you see?" and you'll get a generic description. Upload the same file with this framework and you get a structured analysis that can go directly into a deck.

The framework assigns Claude the role of Senior Data Analyst with a four-stage mandatory structure:

**Layer 1 — Data Overview**

Before any analysis begins, Claude audits the dataset: dimensions, data types, time periods covered, missing values, outliers, sampling bias, and a data completeness score expressed as a percentage of usable records. This layer exists to surface quality issues before they contaminate conclusions. An analysis built on a dataset with 34% missing values in a key field needs a different confidence level than one built on clean data.

**Layer 2 — Key Statistics**

Full descriptive statistics: mean, median, mode, standard deviation, percentiles. Correlation patterns and statistical relationships. Distribution analysis and normality tests. Every anomaly flagged with a confidence interval — not just noted, but quantified.

**Layer 3 — Insights and Findings**

Three to five business insights, ranked by impact potential. Each supported by statistical evidence and effect sizes. Unexpected findings called out separately. Uncertainty levels stated explicitly for every major claim.

**Layer 4 — Recommendations**

Data-driven next steps with expected ROI where estimable. Specific implementation timeline. Explicit identification of data gaps that would strengthen the analysis. All assumptions stated and validation methods suggested.

**Communication standard across all layers:** Business-friendly language with technical depth available on request. Findings ordered by business impact, not analytical sequence. Confidence levels attached to all primary claims.

**How to deploy:** Paste this framework as a system-level instruction before uploading your data file, then write: "Analyse the attached data using this framework." The structure prevents the model from jumping to conclusions and forces it to surface data quality issues before presenting results.

title: /CHAIN OF THOUGHT: The Single Command That Cuts Hallucinations
tagline: Forcing Claude to show its reasoning before delivering a conclusion isn't just a transparency trick — it measurably reduces errors on complex tasks.
content:
Hallucinations in LLMs don't happen randomly. They cluster around tasks that require multi-step reasoning, where the model jumps from input to conclusion without intermediate verification. `/CHAIN OF THOUGHT` breaks that jump into auditable steps.

**What it does:** Forces the model to generate its reasoning process explicitly before producing the final answer. Each inference step appears in sequence, making errors visible before they propagate to the conclusion.

**When to use it:** Complex logical problems. Code with non-obvious control flow. Financial calculations with multiple input variables. Any task where a plausible-sounding wrong answer is worse than a slower right one.

**Why it works technically:** Language models predict the next token based on context. When forced to articulate intermediate reasoning steps, those steps become context for subsequent tokens — creating a self-correcting feedback loop within the response. The model is, in effect, checking its own work as it writes.

**Practical example:** Instead of "Calculate our Q3 hospitality margin given these inputs," write: "/CHAIN OF THOUGHT — Calculate our Q3 hospitality margin. Show each step: revenue components, cost deductions, adjustment factors, then the final margin. Flag any step where an assumption is required."

**The trade-off:** Longer outputs. On simple tasks, the additional reasoning is overhead. Reserve it for high-stakes calculations, logical puzzles, or any output that will be presented to a director without independent verification.

**Combined with `/EVAL-SELF`:** After the chain of thought, add "/EVAL-SELF — identify any step where the reasoning was uncertain or where an alternative interpretation was possible." This surfaces the weak points in the model's own analysis before you act on it.

title: The Sketchnote Prompt: Convert Any Dense Topic into a Visual in One Shot
tagline: A four-variable template that turns complex subjects into professional graphic-recording visuals — for presentations, onboarding, and social content.
content:
Dense topics resist linear documentation. A sketchnote — the graphic recording format used by professional visual thinkers — communicates the same information spatially, making it scannable at a glance and memorable in a way that bullet points are not.

This prompt template produces that output from any AI image generator. Four variables control everything:

**The core prompt structure:**

"First, use well-constructed analogies to clarify [TOPIC]. Relate its principles to everyday experiences. Based on that explanation, create a hand-drawn sketchnote visual summary: black ink fine-liners for outlines, [ACCENT COLOUR] for shading, [TEXT STYLE] text, radially distributed doodles and business icons around a 3D-style central title box, arrows connecting ideas, aspect ratio [RATIO]."

**Variable 1 — Topic:** Free text. Be specific — "GenAI parameters vs context window size" performs better than "AI concepts."

**Variable 2 — Text Style:**
- *Telegraphic* — Nouns and verbs only. Maximum brevity. Best for presentations.
- *Punchy* — Short, high-impact sentences.
- *Scannable* — Heavy headers and bolded keywords.
- *Nugget-sized* — Standalone bite-sized facts, each self-contained.
- *Expansive* — Full explanations for onboarding or educational content.

**Variable 3 — Accent Colour:** Teal, Orange, Muted Red, Emerald, Navy Blue, Mustard, Charcoal, Royal Purple. These eight are pre-tested against black ink line art. Don't guess outside this set.

**Variable 4 — Aspect Ratio:** 16:9 for presentations, 9:16 for Stories and Shorts, 1:1 for social posts, 3:4 for portrait documents.

**Why "radially distributed" matters:** This instruction prevents the output defaulting to a linear list with icons — the generic AI visual. Radial composition produces the whiteboard brainstorm feel: ideas branching outward from a central title, connected by arrows, with natural visual hierarchy.

**High-value use cases:** Process onboarding ("How our data pipeline works" + Scannable + Navy + 16:9). Conference slides ("Zero Trust Architecture" + Telegraphic + Charcoal + 16:9). Social content ("Three things about context windows" + Punchy + Orange + 1:1).

title: Turn Any Photo into a Hand-Drawn Illustration: The Line Art Prompt
tagline: A structured visual prompt that converts photographs into warm, sketch-style illustrations — useful for presentations, reports, and creative communication.
content:
AI image generation is often used for photorealistic outputs. But for professional documents and presentations, a controlled illustrative style is frequently more useful — it's tonally neutral, avoids stock-photo aesthetics, and can be styled to match brand guidelines.

This prompt produces a specific line-art illustration style from any source image: warm, hand-drawn, minimal, with high emotional legibility.

**The prompt structure:**

Step 1 — Inventory the image: list every object, person, or element visible.

Step 2 — Apply this style specification:

"White fine-line strokes on a dark background, natural hand-drawn quality. Lines slightly rough and imperfect — continuous sketch feel, not precise technical drawing. Objects outlined freely, spontaneous and playful. Add directional arrows, small doodles, or dotted lines to guide the eye. Short handwritten-style notes beside each element. Balanced composition with organic structure — like a journal page or scrapbook layout."

**Tonal parameters:** Soft, warm, and inviting. Slightly dreamlike. Emotionally resonant without being heavy. Minimal but with considered detail. Add small decorative elements — hearts, stars, sparkles, small faces — where appropriate to the subject.

**Optional enhancement:** Add a subtle glow effect to make white lines stand out against the background.

**Where this style works well in a professional context:** Persona illustrations for customer research decks. Journey map visualisations where clinical diagrams feel cold. Executive presentation covers where photography feels generic. Onboarding materials where warmth and approachability matter more than precision.

**The key restraint:** Keep it readable. The style should add warmth without obscuring information. If a label or annotation is hard to read, it defeats the purpose.

title: /NO AUTOPILOT: How to Stop Claude from Giving You Generic Outputs
tagline: Every LLM defaults to safe, corporate-sounding language when under-constrained. This command forces it out of that register — and here's what to combine it with.
content:
Claude, like all large language models, has a strong prior toward safe, measured, institutionally acceptable language. It's a product of training on vast amounts of professional text. Left unconstrained, it produces outputs that are competent, inoffensive, and forgettable.

`/NO AUTOPILOT` is a direct instruction to break that pattern. It tells the model that generic outputs are not acceptable — that the response must be specific, original, and avoid clichéd formulations.

**On its own, it's insufficient.** "Don't be generic" without a direction for what to be instead creates ambiguity. The effective combination is `/NO AUTOPILOT` paired with a specific `/TONE` instruction.

**Practical combinations:**

`/NO AUTOPILOT /TONE: direct and opinionated` — Forces the model to take a position rather than hedge. Useful for strategic recommendations where you need a clear point of view, not a balanced summary of considerations.

`/NO AUTOPILOT /TONE: precise and technical` — Removes the tendency to over-explain and pad. Good for documentation, data summaries, or any output going to a technically literate audience.

`/NO AUTOPILOT /TONE: concise and editorial` — Produces writing that reads like it was edited, not generated. Useful for communications, summaries, and anything with an external audience.

**What to expect:** Outputs will be shorter, more direct, and occasionally more assertive than you're used to. The model will make choices rather than presenting all options. Review the first few outputs to calibrate — you may want to modulate the tone instruction based on how far the model moves.

**What not to combine it with:** `/ELI5` or anything that requires simplification. The tension between "don't be generic" and "explain simply" usually resolves in favour of the simplification command, which defeats the purpose.
